exemplar.title: To what extent has ergonomics in shoe insoles catered to people suffering from flat feet?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has ergonomics in shoe insoles catered to people suffering from flat feet?

strengths: Clear and accurate communication of the investigation focus on ergonomic insoles for flat-foot sufferers
Explicit, focused research question consistently linked to discussion
Well-organized methodology showing logical sequence and informed selection of tools and materials
weaknesses: Repetition in the introduction and minor lapses in expression
Omitted details around sampling rationale and validity checks
Truncated interview table entries and missing intermediate process steps
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates topic and purpose very effectively (band 5), states a clear, focused research question (band 5) and presents a mostly complete, justified methodology (upper band 3â4). Taken together, this merits a highâend score of 5/6 for Focus and Method, reflecting strong performance with minor omissions preventing a maximum mark.

strengths: Demonstrates sound subject knowledge of ergonomics, materials and manufacturing methods
Generally accurate use of key design and ergonomics terminology
Relevant source application to justify major design decisions
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on non-scholarly websites and limited depth of biomechanical theory
Occasional terminology errors (e.g., âsiliconâ vs. âsiliconeâ) and informal phrasing
Inconsistent citation style and unsupported claims in places
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and Understanding shows good foundational understanding and mostly accurate terminology (upper band 3â4) but is held back by reliance on popular sources and uneven citation depth. A mid-band score of 4/6 appropriately reflects this level.

strengths: Research is consistently and directly relevant to the research question throughout
Conclusions are logically drawn from the analysis of both secondary and primary data
Discussion identifies key product limitations and proposes constructive improvements
weaknesses: Generalizability is limited by a single-participant study
Lacks quantitative evaluation metrics (e.g., pressure mapping, statistical analysis)
Environmental and economic factors are mentioned only briefly
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Across relevance, analysis and evaluation the work demonstrates sustained coherence with the research question and well-supported conclusions, aligning with the top band (10â12) for Critical Thinking. Despite some singleâcase and quantitative weaknesses, the overall depth and consistency justify the maximum score of 12/12.

strengths: Overall structure follows expected report conventions (title page, TOC, numbered sections)
Clear internal headings and logical flow between sections
weaknesses: Inconsistent font sizes and broken image references
Table of contents has blank entries and mis-indentation
Figures lack captions or correct numbering; tables and lists are misaligned or incomplete
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets the core structural requirements (band 3â4) but suffers from multiple formatting and layout errors that prevent full professionalism (band 1â2). A balanced score of 3/4 reflects solid organization tempered by these execution issues.

strandSelections.reasoning: Pages are numbered and basic formatting features (headings, table, images) are used.  However, inconsistent font sizing, occasional broken image references, and APA citation errors detract from professionalism.  Figures are sometimes blurry or misaligned.  These issues place the work in the 1-2 band, with partial but not fully correct formatting.

Secondary product analysis, medical background information and client interview data are clearly linked to the RQ and inform specification decisions, showing good relevance.  However, research breadth is modest (single user, few academic sources) and environmental or economic analyses are thin, so the work does not reach the sustained excellence required for the 7-9 band.

The essay follows the EE structural conventions: title page, abstract substitute, table of contents, sequentially numbered sections, conclusion and works cited.  Internal headings make navigation easy, though some section ordering is uneven and the table of contents is partially blank.  Overall structure is appropriate and complete, but minor weaknesses keep it at the lower end of the top band.

Discussion includes identification of product limitations, reflection on manufacturing challenges, and suggested improvements, indicating a degree of critical evaluation.  Nonetheless, evaluation criteria are informal, environmental impacts are only briefly considered, and alternative approaches are not rigorously weighed.  The analysis is therefore âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ, aligning with 4-6, score 4.

The research question â âTo what extent has ergonomics in shoe insoles catered to people suffering from flat feet?â â is explicitly stated, specific, and consistently referenced in later sections (secondary research review, interview, testing and evaluation).  Although phrased rather broadly (it mixes âextentâ with a single case-study prototype), it nonetheless guides the discussion and data collection in a focused way.  The clear linkage between RQ, methodology, and analysis warrants placement in the top band, but the slight breadth/ambiguity justifies a score of 5 rather than 6.

The essay demonstrates sound knowledge of basic ergonomic principles (arch support, force distribution), materials (LSR-2 silicone, modelling clay) and manufacturing methods.  Secondary sources are cited, and information is applied to justify design choices.  Nevertheless, many sources are popular websites, several claims are unsupported, and there is limited depth in biomechanical theory.  Source integration is sometimes descriptive rather than analytical, positioning the work in the middle band.

Key DT termsâanthropometrics, ergonomics, biomechanical factors, one-off production, injection mouldingâare used consistently and generally accurately.  Occasional misuse (e.g., âsiliconâ instead of âsiliconeâ, âcowboy productsâ) and some informal phrasing prevent full consistency, but overall terminology and conceptual language show adequate control, justifying the upper end of the 3-4 band.

The student analyses causes of flat feet, existing products, and prototype test results, drawing logical but mainly qualitative conclusions.  Some quantitative evidence (foot dimensions) is present yet not statistically processed.  Conclusions about market gaps and prototype effectiveness are sensible but rely heavily on one participant.  Depth is therefore moderate, meriting the lower half of the 4-6 band.

The student clearly identifies the focus on ergonomic shoe-insoles for flat-foot sufferers in both the title and opening paragraph, immediately explaining why the issue is relevant and what the investigation intends to show.  The introduction contextualises flat feet, links ergonomics to user comfort and states that the essay will evaluate existing and new solutions.  Throughout the report all major sections (introduction, causes, comparison, design opportunity, specification, etc.) stay aligned with this central purpose, demonstrating accurate and effective topic communication.  Minor lapses in expression and some repetition prevent the work from reaching the very top of the band.

The student describes a mixed-methods procedure (literature review, product benchmarking, client interview, anthropometric measurement, CAD modelling, prototyping, user testing) that is logically ordered and mostly complete.  Evidence of informed selection is shown through justification of silicone, CAD software and measurement techniques.  However, the methodology lacks detail on sampling (single client only), validity checks, and criteria for evaluating success; data-collection instruments are not included in full.  These omissions limit reliability, so the work fits comfortably in the 3-4 band, at the upper level.

annotations.comment: The title clearly communicates the focus on ergonomic insoles and flat-foot users, giving a precise context for the investigation.
The research question is explicitly stated and focused, guiding subsequent sections effectively.
Introduction repeats aims in each paragraph; consider condensing redundant statements to sharpen focus and avoid repetition.
Time-plan table lists tasks but lacks explicit links to research milestones; add a column mapping tasks to RQ objectives for coherence.
Word count is provided and under the 4 000âword limit, meeting IB formatting requirements.
Table of contents includes blank entries and inconsistent indentation; ensure all sections have page numbers and clear hierarchy.
Citation â(Sandhya)â lacks full bibliographic detail and consistent style; provide complete author, date, and source in text or footnote.
Methodology overview is logical but omits sampling rationale and validity checks; specify participant selection and success criteria to strengthen reliability.
Figure is unnumbered and lacks caption; add a descriptive caption like âFig 1 â Foot arch comparisonâ beneath the image for clarity.
Embedded reference â(Fig 2.)â precedes the figure location; ensure all figures are sequentially numbered and correctly placed near mentions.
Heading âRO Relevanceâ appears to be a typo; correct to âRQ Relevanceâ to maintain consistency with the research question acronym.
Comparison analysis is descriptive and lacks reference to external biomechanical data; integrate scholarly sources to support your interpretations.
The tableâs analysis text is lengthy; summarise key contrasts in bullet points to enhance clarity and reader engagement.
Secondary research relies on market claims; bolster credibility by including peer-reviewed studies on insole efficacy.
Inline dimensions use raw math tags. Format as $$11.43\times3.02\times31.24\,{\rm cm}$$ to match academic conventions.
Interview figure is unlabeled and uncaptioned; add âFig X: Semi-structured interview setupâ and describe key elements in the caption.
Using a single participant limits generalisability; discuss this sampling limitation or expand your user testing to strengthen conclusions.
Interview table entries for questions 3 and 4 are truncated and unclear; ensure full client responses are captured and presented.
Link your interview findings directly to specific design requirements in this concluding paragraph to tighten alignment with the RQ.
Terminology error: âsiliconâ should be âsilicone.â Use precise material names to avoid confusion.
The phrase âcowboy productsâ is informal; replace with formal terminology (e.g., âsubstandardâ or âlow-qualityâ) for professional tone.
Design specifications table is misaligned and contains empty cells; reorganise rows and columns so each criterion has a clear description.
Marketing specs list breaks across pages; combine all bullets in one list to maintain continuity and prevent reader confusion.
User-needs section is descriptive; consider quantifying requirements (e.g., hours of wear or weight capacity) to create measurable specifications.
Sentence fragments (âIt was measurementsâ¦â) disrupt readability; revise for grammatical coherence and logical flow.
Material-property list for LSR-2 is comprehensive; prioritise those properties directly impacting insole performance to maintain focus.
The text refers to âFig 13, 14, 15â but only one CAD image is shown; update figure references or include the missing visuals.
Process steps omit intermediate actions (e.g., demoulding and curing durations); include all stages for a complete methodology.
Procedural list details steps but lacks embedded reflections on challenges; integrate brief evaluations after each key stage.
Product-making table ends abruptly after initial tasks; expand the table to include evidence for all manufacturing and testing steps.
Challenge and problem-solving narrative is dense; use bullet points and subheadings to distinguish each challenge and your solution.
Final insole image lacks annotations; add arrows or callouts to highlight ergonomic features such as arch location and cushioning zones.
Client feedback is wholly qualitative; include structured metrics (e.g., pressure mapping or comfort scales) for more rigorous evaluation.
Client feedback list identifies defects; propose precise corrective actions linked to material or process adjustments.
Reflection notes ensuring clay hardening next timeâhow will you test or verify this improvement in future prototypes?
Problem-solving section suggests using lube but omits assessment of potential side effects; evaluate viability and safety of this improvement.
Work cited entries show inconsistent formatting (italics, punctuation) and missing retrieval dates; standardize citations per APA or MLA style.
URLs break across lines with hyphenation errors; ensure each link is unbroken and hyperlinked correctly for accessibility.
